title: Geographical Skills: Fieldwork and Enquiry
examTip: Fieldwork questions on Paper 3 ask about YOUR fieldwork investigations — but they also ask general methods questions. Know the enquiry process from start to finish: question → hypothesis → method → data → analysis → conclusion → evaluation.
introTips: Know the full enquiry sequence: question → risk assessment → sampling → data collection → presentation → analysis → conclusion → evaluation.
For evaluation questions: reliability (would repeating give same results?), validity (did methods measure what you intended?), improvements (larger sample, more sites, different season).
Bipolar analysis is a key human geography fieldwork method — know how it works.
intro: Fieldwork is where geography gets real. Instead of just reading about how rivers deposit sediment or how environmental quality changes from the CBD to the suburbs, you go and measure it yourself. The enquiry process — from asking a good question all the way to honestly evaluating your methods — is a core skill that AQA tests directly on Paper 3. Understanding how to plan, collect, present and evaluate geographical data is as important as knowing the content.

keyPoints.heading: Planning a geographical enquiry
keyPoints.content: A good geographical enquiry starts with a clear enquiry question — it must be geographical, testable and at an appropriate scale. Examples: 'How does pebble size vary with distance along Chesil Beach?' or 'How does environmental quality vary between the CBD and inner city in Bristol?' A hypothesis is a testable statement you can attempt to prove or disprove: 'Pebble size decreases from east to west along Chesil Beach.' Before fieldwork, a risk assessment is essential: identify specific hazards (traffic on busy roads, slippery riverbanks, deep water, sunburn, cold); rate their likelihood and severity; state control measures (hi-vis jackets, buddy system, waterproof clothing, risk forms signed). Choosing your sampling strategy before collecting data is critical: random (every location has equal probability — use a random number generator for grid references); systematic (regular intervals — e.g., every 10m along a transect); stratified (proportional to subgroups — e.g., 50% male, 50% female respondents for a questionnaire).
writeBullets: Enquiry question — geographical, testable, appropriate scale
Hypothesis — a testable statement; e.g., 'Pebble size decreases from east to west along Chesil Beach'
Risk assessment — identify hazards, rate likelihood/severity, state control measures
Random sampling — equal probability; random number generator
Systematic sampling — regular intervals (e.g., every 10m along a transect)
Stratified sampling — proportional to subgroups (e.g., 50% male, 50% female)
keyPoints.memTip: QHRSS — Question, Hypothesis, Risk, Sampling Strategy. Four steps before you set foot outside.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often confuse random and systematic sampling. Random = truly random (number generator); systematic = regular intervals (every 10m, every 5th person).

keyPoints.heading: Physical geography fieldwork methods
keyPoints.content: Physical fieldwork typically involves measuring natural processes. River fieldwork: velocity — use a flowmeter (measures speed in m/s directly) or float method (time a float over 10m; velocity = 10 ÷ time × 0.8 as a correction factor for surface vs average speed); width — tape measure stretched across the channel at the water's surface; depth — metre ruler placed vertically at regular intervals across the channel width (used to calculate cross-sectional area = width × average depth); bedload size and roundness — measure and record the longest axis of stones at regular sample points; use Powers' roundness scale (1 = very angular to 6 = very rounded) to assess how much stones have been worn by attrition; turbidity — water cloudiness measured using a Secchi disk or turbidity tube. Coastal fieldwork: wave count (number of waves per minute at a set point); swash and backwash distances (how far water reaches up and down the beach face); pebble size and roundness at different points along the beach; beach profile (measure angle of slope with a clinometer at regular intervals up the beach face).
writeBullets: River velocity — flowmeter (m/s) or float method (10m ÷ time × 0.8)
River width — tape measure across channel at water surface
River depth — metre ruler at regular intervals; used to calculate cross-section area
Bedload — measure longest axis; Powers' roundness scale (1 angular → 6 rounded)
Turbidity — water cloudiness; Secchi disk or turbidity tube
Coastal: wave count, swash/backwash distances, pebble size, beach profile with clinometer
keyPoints.memTip: River: Velocity, Width, Depth, Bedload, Turbidity — VWDBT. Five river measurements.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often forget the 0.8 correction factor for the float method — the float travels at surface speed, but average river velocity is about 80% of this.

keyPoints.heading: Human geography fieldwork methods
keyPoints.content: Questionnaire design: use closed questions (yes/no, multiple choice — easy to quantify and analyse) alongside some open questions (allow detailed responses — harder to analyse but reveal nuance). Avoid leading questions ('Don't you think the area is dirty?' — suggests the answer). Always conduct a pilot survey (test with 5–10 people first to identify problems). For sampling respondents: use systematic sampling (e.g., every 5th person who passes). Land use survey: walk a transect through the study area recording the use of each building (residential, retail, commercial, industrial, vacant, derelict) on a pre-prepared recording sheet. Pedestrian and traffic counts: count at set intervals (e.g., 5 minutes every hour) at multiple locations simultaneously to compare footfall between areas. Environmental quality survey (bipolar analysis): rate multiple criteria on a scale from −3 to +3 (e.g., litter: −3 = lots of litter, +3 = very clean; noise: −3 = very noisy, +3 = very quiet; green space, graffiti, building condition). Total the scores for each location and compare — the method allows objective comparison of subjective conditions.
writeBullets: Questionnaire — closed questions (quantifiable) + open questions (qualitative); pilot survey
Avoid leading questions — suggest the expected answer and bias responses
Systematic respondent sampling — every 5th person; reduces interviewer bias
Land use survey — transect through area; record each building type on sheet
Pedestrian/traffic count — count at set intervals (5 min/hour) at multiple sites
Bipolar analysis — rate criteria on −3 to +3 scale; allows objective comparison
keyPoints.memTip: QLPB — Questionnaire, Land use, Pedestrian count, Bipolar. Four human geography fieldwork tools.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Bipolar analysis is the key environmental quality method — students need to understand it rates each criterion on a scale so locations can be compared objectively.

keyPoints.heading: Presenting fieldwork data
keyPoints.content: Choosing the right presentation technique for your data type is itself a skill. Quantitative data: bar charts (compare sites or categories), line graphs (show change over distance or time), scatter graphs (investigate relationships), histograms (frequency distribution), pie charts (proportions). Spatial/locational data: located bar graphs or pie charts placed on a map at the collection sites; choropleth maps (shading areas by value); isoline maps (contour-style lines connecting equal values — used for temperature or rainfall data). Field sketches: an annotated sketch of a landscape drawn in the field — key geographical features are labelled with arrows explaining the process involved (e.g., an arrow pointing to a cliff face labelled 'hydraulic action and abrasion forming undercut'). Annotated photographs: photograph with labelled arrows identifying key features or processes. Tables of raw data: clearly labelled with column headers and units — the starting point for all further analysis.
writeBullets: Quantitative data — bar, line, scatter, histogram, pie charts
Spatial data — located pie/bar charts on maps; choropleth; isoline maps
Field sketch — annotated landscape drawing showing geographical features and processes
Annotated photograph — arrows and labels identifying features and processes
Tables of raw data — clearly labelled with units; foundation for analysis
Match presentation type to data type — choose carefully
keyPoints.memTip: Quantitative → chart; Spatial → map; Landscape → sketch. Match method to data type.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Field sketches are underused — they allow annotation of process in a way photographs cannot. Encourage students to practise annotating landscape sketches.

keyPoints.heading: Analysing, concluding and evaluating
keyPoints.content: Analysis means describing patterns in your data and explaining why they exist, using geographical knowledge of processes. Example: 'Pebble size generally decreases from east to west along Chesil Beach — because longshore drift moves material westwards and attrition gradually reduces pebble size over time.' Use statistical measures (mean, median, range, IQR) to describe the data numerically and support your description. Conclusion: do your results support the hypothesis? State clearly whether the evidence supports, partially supports, or contradicts your hypothesis. Relate findings back to the original enquiry question. Suggest wider implications or how findings relate to geographical theory. Evaluation: reliability — would repeating the fieldwork give similar results? How could you check? (cross-check with peer data, repeat on different day). Validity — did your methods actually measure what you intended? (e.g., was your questionnaire well-designed? Were enough locations sampled?). Improvements: larger sample size, more sampling locations, different time of year or weather conditions, longer time at each site, peer checking of data collection.
writeBullets: Analysis — describe pattern, use statistics, explain using geographical processes
Quote data in analysis — 'Mean pebble size at Site 1 was 4.2cm vs 1.8cm at Site 5'
Conclusion — does evidence support the hypothesis? State clearly; explain why
Reliability — would repeating give the same results? How to check?
Validity — did methods measure what was intended?
Improvements — larger sample, more sites, different season, peer checking
keyPoints.memTip: ACE — Analysis (pattern + explanation), Conclusion (support hypothesis?), Evaluation (reliability + validity + improvements).
keyPoints.tutor_note: The evaluation section is where students most commonly lose marks — generic 'take more samples' is not enough; they need to explain what specific issue this would address.

tables.title: Primary data collection methods — advantages and disadvantages
rows: Flowmeter (river)
Velocity in m/s at different points across channel
Accurate and objective; fast to use
Expensive equipment; can be inaccurate in very shallow water
Float method (river)
Surface velocity (time float over 10m)
Cheap; requires only a stopwatch; easy to repeat
Measures surface speed only; 0.8 correction factor must be applied
Pebble sampling (coastal/river)
Size (longest axis in cm) and roundness (Powers' scale)
Simple; cheap; directly measures process evidence
Subjective element (roundness judgement); small sample may not be representative
Bipolar analysis (human)
Environmental quality scores (−3 to +3) for multiple criteria
Allows objective comparison of different sites; covers multiple factors
Criteria choice is subjective; different surveyors may rate the same feature differently
Questionnaire (human)
Attitudes, opinions, demographic data
Gathers information impossible to observe directly; large sample possible
Leading questions bias results; people may give untruthful answers; pilot survey needed
Land use survey (human)
Type of use for each building along a transect
Quick to do; generates large dataset; objective if categories defined clearly
Categories can overlap; snap-shot in time only; buildings may have multiple uses

0.question: What is the difference between systematic and stratified sampling?
options: Systematic means sampling at regular intervals (e.g., every 10m); stratified means dividing the population into groups and sampling proportionally from each
Systematic means using a random number generator; stratified means measuring every single item in a defined area
Systematic means choosing samples by convenience; stratified means only sampling at the busiest times of day
They are the same method — both involve choosing every 5th person or location in a dataset
0.correctFeedback: Correct! Systematic = regular intervals (every 10m along a transect, every 5th person); stratified = dividing into subgroups and sampling proportionally (e.g., if 60% of visitors are female, 60% of questionnaire respondents should be female). They are very different approaches.
0.wrongFeedback: Not quite. Systematic sampling uses regular intervals (e.g., every 10m along a transect). Stratified sampling divides the population into subgroups and samples proportionally from each — so if 60% of visitors are female, 60% of questionnaires target females.

4.question: In a fieldwork evaluation, what does 'validity' mean?
options: Whether the methods actually measured what they were intended to measure
Whether the results would be the same if the fieldwork was repeated by different people
Whether the sample size was large enough to be statistically significant
Whether the fieldwork was conducted safely with a proper risk assessment
4.correctFeedback: Correct! Validity asks: did your methods measure what you intended? For example, if you used a questionnaire to measure environmental quality, was it actually measuring quality of the environment, or just people's personal preferences? That's a validity question.
4.wrongFeedback: Not quite. Validity means: did your methods actually measure what you intended to measure? (The question of whether repeating would give the same results is reliability — a different concept.)

commonMistakes: Confusing reliability and validity — reliability = would repeating give same results? Validity = did methods measure what you intended? They are different concepts that require different improvements.

Generic evaluation answers like 'take more samples' — always explain what problem this addresses: 'Increasing the sample from 20 to 50 pebbles per site would make the mean more representative and reduce the impact of unusual individual pebbles.'
Forgetting the 0.8 correction factor for the float method — the float travels at surface velocity, but you need average velocity; multiplying by 0.8 accounts for this difference.
Not identifying sampling strategy before data collection — choosing random, systematic or stratified is done before fieldwork, not afterwards; this is a planning step.

Conclusions that just restate the results — a conclusion must answer the hypothesis ('The results support the hypothesis that pebble size decreases from east to west') AND explain why using geographical processes.
examTips: Paper 3 fieldwork questions follow a pattern: you'll be asked about your own investigations. Know your two fieldwork investigations (one physical, one human) inside out: the question, hypothesis, method, presentation technique, conclusion and two evaluative points each.

For evaluation, always give a specific improvement and explain why it would improve reliability or validity — not just 'I would take more samples' but 'I would increase sample size from 20 to 50 per site to reduce the impact of anomalous readings on the mean.'

Bipolar analysis is a key method to know for human geography fieldwork — describe how it rates each criterion on a −3 to +3 scale and explain that this allows objective comparison of subjective environmental quality.
When describing fieldwork methods, always specify: where you measured (location), how many times (sample size), what equipment you used, and how you recorded the data — examiners reward precision.
revisionCardBullets: Enquiry sequence: question → hypothesis → risk assessment → sampling → data → analysis → conclusion → evaluation
Hypothesis — testable statement; 'Pebble size decreases from east to west along Chesil Beach'
Random = random number generator; Systematic = regular intervals; Stratified = proportional subgroups
River: velocity (flowmeter or float × 0.8), width (tape), depth (ruler), bedload (Powers' scale), turbidity
Powers' roundness scale — 1 (angular) to 6 (very rounded); measures attrition of stones
Bipolar analysis — rate criteria −3 to +3; allows objective comparison of environmental quality
Questionnaire: closed + open questions; pilot survey; avoid leading questions
Choropleth maps and isoline maps for spatial data presentation
Field sketches — annotated landscape drawing; process labels on arrows
Analysis: pattern → statistical evidence → geographical explanation
Reliability — would repeating give same results? Cross-check; repeat on another day
Validity — did methods measure what you intended? Evaluate questionnaire design, sample size, locations
